reason: Analog computers process continuous physical quantities directly; input is never digitized.
Modern machines use pipelining/superscalar parallelism while still honoring sequential program order, so both.
ENIAC was designed by J. Presper Eckert and John Mauchly at UPenn; von Neumann joined later for EDVAC.
Dennis Ritchie created C at Bell Labs; Wirth did Pascal, Papert Logo, Knuth TeX/TAOCP.
Peopleware is the standard term for the human component alongside hardware and software.
IBM and Apple formed the AIM alliance (Taligent/Kaleida) in 1991.
"Sillico Sapiens" is a coined nickname for the computer (silicon-based thinker).
Glitch is the alternative jargon for a bug/fault; leech, squid, slug are not.
Reliable but not infallible is the standard phrasing; the other options contradict "very reliable" framing.
Reverse video inverts foreground/background to highlight a screen region needing attention.
Standard answer for the original IBM-PC video display contribution is Taiwan.
The main circuit board holding the chips is the motherboard.
CPU, expansion slots and active components sit on the motherboard.
A dedicated computer is one assigned to a single task/function.
Microprocessor, disk controller and serial interface are inside the system unit; the modem is classically an external/add-on device.
A compiler translates the entire source program to machine code at once.
An interpreter translates and executes one statement at a time.
A smart/intelligent device is called so because it embeds a microcomputer (microprocessor); a sensor provides sensing, not intelligence.
The control unit issues the commands that drive the ALU.
Read, read-out and read-from are all accepted names for retrieving stored data.
